framing: Begin where the Buddha began. The first sermon — the four noble truths and the eightfold path. The structural foundation of everything that follows. If you read only one sutta, read this.

Right reliance. Do not accept teachings on the basis of authority, tradition, or reasoning alone. Test them in your own experience. The Buddha's instruction on how to evaluate any teaching — including his own.
The four foundations of mindfulness — body, feeling-tone, mind, and phenomena. The complete framework for vipassanā. Read it slowly. Many practitioners return to this sutta for years.
Mindfulness of breathing as a complete path. Sixteen steps grouped as four tetrads — each tetrad cultivating one foundation. The breath, properly attended to, contains the whole teaching.
The five hindrances as obstructions to wisdom — like dirty water in which one cannot see one's own reflection. Naming them is the first step in their removal. You will use this map daily.
Five methods for handling unwholesome thoughts as they arise. The Buddha's practical instruction for the work of right effort. Have these methods ready before you need them.
The simile of the adze handle. You cannot see the wearing happen day by day — only after months and years. Trust the development. Use the adze.

The Buddha describes his own pre-awakening practice — sorting his thoughts into two piles. The teaching most relevant to the practitioner who feels the work intensify as they advance: this is what the path actually feels like.
Five qualities of right speech: spoken at the right time, in truth, gently, beneficially, and with metta. Without all five, speech causes harm. The fifth limb of the noble eightfold path.
What feeds the hindrances and what feeds the awakening factors. Wise attention — yoniso manasikāra — is the food that starves doubt and feeds sati. The hidden mechanism behind the entire practice.
